name: Jagadeesh R
bio:
Jagadeesh R works with photography and text to examine habitation, loss and the residue of domestic life. Her practice centres on the photograph as an act of witnessing — not documentation but attention. She is drawn to interior spaces: rooms after people have left them, objects that outlast their owners, light that continues to fall through windows that no longer hold any particular meaning.

Her books are made in small editions of 50–100 copies, hand-assembled in her studio in Delhi. Each is conceived as an object as much as a publication — something to be held, marked, returned to.

Nasser studied at the National Institute of Design, Ahmedabad, and later at the École nationale supérieure des beaux-arts de Paris. She has shown work at the Kiran Nadar Museum of Art, the Serendipity Arts Festival, and in group exhibitions across South Asia and Europe.

name: Aryan Kapoor
bio:
Aryan Kapoor makes photographic documents of temporary dwelling — hotels, rental rooms, transit spaces. His practice spans twelve years and four cities. He is interested in the room as a unit of evidence: what a space reveals about the lives lived inside it once those lives have moved on.

54 Rooms, his first book with dadada, is the culmination of a decade of photographing rooms the morning after his last night in them. No people appear. Only the traces: the shape of sleep in an unmade bed, the particular yellow of a rented ceiling, a window facing the wrong direction for the light.

Kapoor works as an architect by day. His photographic practice exists in parallel — slower, more personal, less interested in resolution.

name: Siddharth Rao
bio:
Siddharth Rao makes video and audio works that excavate histories through listening — field recordings, interviews, radio archives and ambient sound. He is interested in what recording preserves and what it loses: the gap between a sound and its capture.

Border Radio emerged from two years of work along the India-Pakistan border. Rao recorded not at the official checkpoints but in the spaces between — the fields, tea shops, and abandoned radio rooms that line the edge. The testimonies woven through the work come from radio operators who were present during the 1971 conflict.

Rao studied sound design at the Film and Television Institute of India, Pune. His work has been shown at the Experimenta Media Arts Festival, the Dhaka Art Summit, and in several sound art contexts internationally.

name: Anjali Sood
bio:
Anjali Sood keeps a photographic diary. Her practice is rooted in duration — returning to the same places across years and seasons to record change that is too slow to notice in a single visit but unmistakable across time.

Monsoon Journal began in 2017 as a personal project: to photograph the same streets of North Kolkata across every monsoon season. Seven years later it has become something else — a record of a neighbourhood transforming, of a city learning to live with water, of the particular way rain changes what is visible.

Sood is self-taught. She works as a secondary school teacher and makes photographs in the early mornings before school begins.

name: Rahul Das
bio:
Rahul Das photographs the working environments of small-scale industry in and around Howrah. His work is slow, attentive and deeply local — made in foundries and workshops that produce components for industries the world depends on but rarely photographs.

Das is interested in the politics of visibility: who gets documented, who gets aestheticised, who gets ignored. His photographs resist the picturesque. They are not beautiful in any easy sense. They are patient, precise, and quietly insistent that this work and these workers deserve the same sustained attention that photography usually reserves for other subjects.

Das studied commerce. He began photographing in 2008 after inheriting a camera from his uncle.

name: Mira Johal
bio:
Mira Johal is an installation artist working with landscape, time and ecological change. Her works are often site-specific and built around long-duration observation — months or years of returning to a single place to record its transformations.

A Long Season emerged from eighteen months of visits to a single field in Punjab. Johal photographed the field every three weeks, always at different times of day, allowing the work to accumulate a portrait of agricultural time that is at once documentary and elegiac.

Johal has exhibited at the Kochi-Muziris Biennale, the Serendipity Arts Festival, and in solo and group shows across India and the UK. She teaches at the Chandigarh College of Architecture.

name: Noor Fatima
bio:
Noor Fatima works across Pakistan and India with photographic archives of Partition. Her practice is collaborative, often working with families to reconstruct lost image collections — photographs that were separated when their owners were separated in 1947.

Archive Fever is her most sustained project: a two-city exhibition and publication that attempts to digitally reunite photographs physically divided by the border. The work is both archival and deeply personal — Fatima's own family was split between Lahore and Delhi.

Fatima trained as an archivist and holds degrees from Lahore University of Management Sciences and the School of Oriental and African Studies, London.

name: Priya Menon
bio:
Priya Menon is a researcher and visual artist whose practice hovers between art history and speculative fiction. She is interested in things that were not documented — exhibitions that happened and left no record, performances that were never photographed, archives that were destroyed or never created.

Grey City is her first photographic work in a practice that has until now been primarily text-based. The project grew from a habit of early morning walks through Chennai — photographs made before the city woke up, before the light became familiar, before the streets became themselves again.

Menon holds a PhD in art history from Jawaharlal Nehru University and teaches visual studies at a college in Chennai.

name: Vikram Nair
bio:
Vikram Nair grew up in Kutch and has spent much of his adult life photographing the Rann — the vast salt marsh that transforms from sea to land to salt flat across the seasons. His relationship with the landscape is intimate and generational; his family has lived at its edge for four generations.

Salt Flat is the result of a decade of sustained attention: a book about geological time and the communities that have worked the salt for centuries. The agariyas — the salt farmers who live on the Rann for eight months of the year, in houses built from salt — are its central subjects.

Nair is largely self-taught. He has received support from the India Foundation for the Arts and has exhibited in India, Germany and the United States.

name: Reema Das
bio:
Reema Das makes observational video works about labour, time and the body. Her films are made over extended periods — weeks or months spent in a single location, watching and waiting for the ordinary to become visible.

Night Shift was filmed over six weeks in a tea processing factory in Assam, always between 10pm and 6am. Das was interested in the quality of attention that nighttime work demands — a particular kind of focus that has no spectacle, only duration.

Das studied film at Satyajit Ray Film and Television Institute, Kolkata. Her work has been screened at the Mumbai International Film Festival, the Singapore International Film Festival, and in gallery contexts across Asia.

name: Aditi Roy
bio:
Aditi Roy makes image essays that document lives lived in ecological precarity. Her practice is built on long-term relationships with communities — she does not parachute in to photograph disaster but returns over years to record the rhythms of ordinary life against the backdrop of recurring crisis.

Flood Plain began in 2019 when Roy met three families living on the Brahmaputra flood plain. She has returned every year since, photographing the floods, the recovery, and the extraordinary pragmatism with which these families inhabit a landscape that is never quite stable.

Roy is based between Guwahati and Delhi. She has received fellowships from the Magnum Foundation and the Pulitzer Center.

name: Kiran Rao
bio:
Kiran Rao is a photographer working in and around Bellary, Karnataka. His practice focuses on the iron ore mining industry and its transformation of the landscape — a story that is simultaneously geological, economic and deeply human.

Red Earth was made between 2018 and 2022, during and after the Supreme Court's interventions in the Bellary mining sector. Rao photographed the mines, the miners, the trucks, and the red dust that settles on everything within twenty kilometres of the excavations.

Rao studied journalism and began photographing while working as a reporter. He lives in Bellary with his family.

name: Sunil Mehta
bio:
Sunil Mehta is a typographer, printer and visual artist whose practice moves between making things and documenting the disappearance of the trades that made things. He has spent thirty years working in Pune's printing industry and twenty photographing its decline.

Paper Town is an essay about the last offset printing presses of Narayan Peth — a neighbourhood that once printed the books, pamphlets, wedding invitations and underground newspapers of Maharashtra. The work is both documentary and elegiac; Mehta printed the book himself on one of the machines he photographed.

Mehta is a founding member of the Pune Typography Collective and has lectured on print history at institutions across India.

name: Farida Khan
bio:
Farida Khan makes video works about ritual, performance and the politics of spectacle. She is interested in ceremonies that serve double functions — as genuine cultural practice and as theatre for an audience, domestic and international.

The Crossing emerged from a year of filming the Wagah border ceremony — the daily flag-lowering ritual at the India-Pakistan border that has become a major tourist attraction on both sides. Khan filmed every performance for a year, accumulating 365 sunsets, before compressing them into 28 minutes.

Khan studied fine art at the National College of Arts, Lahore, and completed an MFA at Goldsmith's College, London. Her work has been shown in Pakistan, India, the UK and the United States.

name: Dev Sharma
bio:
Dev Sharma is a photographer and writer based in Shimla. His practice centres on the hill stations of the Western Himalayas — the colonial-era towns built by the British as summer capitals, now living complicated second and third lives as domestic tourist destinations.

Winter Notes began as a personal project: to photograph Shimla in the months after the tourists left, when the town returned to its residents. Over four winters, Sharma built a portrait of a place that is always slightly out of season — architecturally, climatically, historically.

Sharma has published two previous books of photographs and writes regularly on photography and place for several Indian publications.
